bonuses.leadin: This poet used the phrase “Northern Dedications” to mock his rival’s flattery of the Duke of Newcastle. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poet used the phrase "Northern Dedications" to mock his rival's flattery of the Duke of Newcastle. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poet of the satirical poem Mac Flecknoe, which sees his real-life contemporary Thomas Shadwell become the new ruler of the “realms of Non-sense.” This author was the first Poet Laureate of England.
Dryden and Shadwell disagreed over the genius of this author, who wrote a love song that asks the title woman to “leave a kiss but in the cup / And I’ll not look for wine.”
Dryden also wrote this poem in which the Duke of Monmouth and the Earl of Shaftesbury are satirized as the two title Biblical figures rebelling against David.
parts_sanitized: Name this poet of the satirical poem Mac Flecknoe, which sees his real-life contemporary Thomas Shadwell become the new ruler of the "realms of Non-sense." This author was the first Poet Laureate of England.
Dryden and Shadwell disagreed over the genius of this author, who wrote a love song that asks the title woman to "leave a kiss but in the cup / And I'll not look for wine."
Dryden also wrote this poem in which the Duke of Monmouth and the Earl of Shaftesbury are satirized as the two title Biblical figures rebelling against David.
answers: Ben Jonson [or Benjamin Jonson] (The poem is “Song: To Celia.”)
answers_sanitized: Ben Jonson [or Benjamin Jonson] (The poem is "Song: To Celia.")

bonuses.leadin: This poet “stands confirm’d in full stupidity,” and “never deviates into sense.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this “True-blue Protestant poet,” who is satirized in a mock-heroic work beginning “All human things are subject to decay” in which he solemnly swears to remain dull and is crowned by the king Nonsense.
In addition to mocking Shadwell in “MacFlecknoe,” this poet wrote about Palmyra and Leonidas in Marriage a la Mode and described his conversion to Catholicism in “The Hind and the Panther.”
Dryden satirized the rebellion of the Earl of Shaftsbury and the Duke of Monmouth, represented as the two title Biblical characters rebelling against David in this monarchic poem.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poet "stands confirm'd in full stupidity," and "never deviates into sense." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this "True-blue Protestant poet," who is satirized in a mock-heroic work beginning "All human things are subject to decay" in which he solemnly swears to remain dull and is crowned by the king Nonsense.
In addition to mocking Shadwell in "MacFlecknoe," this poet wrote about Palmyra and Leonidas in Marriage a la Mode and described his conversion to Catholicism in "The Hind and the Panther."
Dryden satirized the rebellion of the Earl of Shaftsbury and the Duke of Monmouth, represented as the two title Biblical characters rebelling against David in this monarchic poem.

bonuses.leadin: This poem begins with the line “in pious times, ere priestcraft did begin.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this allegory that uses the story of King David to satirize the Popish plot and the Monmouth Rebellion.
This Restoration author wrote Absalom and Achitophel as well as MacFlecknoe.
After John Dryden converted to Catholicism, he wrote this work in which he compared the Quakers to hares and the Presbyterians to wolves.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poem begins with the line "in pious times, ere priestcraft did begin." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this allegory that uses the story of King David to satirize the Popish plot and the Monmouth Rebellion.
This Restoration author wrote Absalom and Achitophel as well as MacFlecknoe.
After John Dryden converted to Catholicism, he wrote this work in which he compared the Quakers to hares and the Presbyterians to wolves.

bonuses.leadin: The second part of this poem lampoons Thomas Shadwell and Elkanah Settle as Og and Doeg. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this political satire by John Dryden, which allegorizes Charles II as King David and represents Shaftsbury and Monmouth as the two title characters.

Dryden commissioned this man to help him write the second part of Abaslom and Acitophel. This English poet laureate wrote a more cheerful King Lear that outstripped the popularity of Shakespeare’s until the 18th century, as well as the libretto to Dido and Aeneas.
In Absalom and Achitophel, this author is depicted as the servant Zimri. He had angered Dryden by mocking his style in his satirical play The Rehearsal.
answers_sanitized: George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham [accept either]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The second part of this poem lampoons Thomas Shadwell and Elkanah Settle as Og and Doeg. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this political satire by John Dryden, which allegorizes Charles II as King David and represents Shaftsbury and Monmouth as the two title characters.

Dryden commissioned this man to help him write the second part of Abaslom and Acitophel. This English poet laureate wrote a more cheerful King Lear that outstripped the popularity of Shakespeare's until the 18th century, as well as the libretto to Dido and Aeneas.
In Absalom and Achitophel, this author is depicted as the servant Zimri. He had angered Dryden by mocking his style in his satirical play The Rehearsal.
